preamble: The shift from the voiced alveolar stop [d] to the voiced postalveolar fricative [ʒ] (zh) is a form of palatalization and spirantization. It typically occurs when [d] is followed by a palatal semivowel [j] (yod-coalescence), passing through an intermediate affricate stage [dʒ] before losing its stop component.

examples.note: Historical palatalization: Latin /dj/ strengthened to [dʒ] in Old French and subsequently deaffricated to [ʒ] in Modern French.
Spirantization of the standard affricate [dʒ] into a pure fricative [ʒ] in rapid or informal speech.

sources: Alkire, T., & Rosen, C. (2010). Romance Philology.
Wells, J. C. (1982). Accents of English.
